content:
7 min read
Just now

--

“We believe good men more fully and more readily than others: this is true generally whatever the question is, and absolutely true where exact certainty is impossible and opinions are divided.” ~ Aristotle ~

Two thousand years ago, Aristotle wasn’t writing about software. He was describing ethos, the observation that persuasion rarely rests on the strength of an argument alone. It rests on how much we trust the person making it. And he adds a precise condition for when this matters most: not when certainty is easy to check, but when it isn’t and when the truth is hard to verify and reasonable people could disagree. In exactly those moments, we stop evaluating the claim and start evaluating the source.
When AI says “this is production-ready,” most people have no way to check whether that’s true. “Is this code production-ready?” is precisely the kind of question that is not easy to verify. It requires knowing what a production system must survive (such as load, adversarial input, edge cases, failure) and most people asking the question have no independent way to check the answer themselves. So they do what Aristotle says people have always done: they default to the credibility of whoever just answered them.
The problem is that an AI’s answer arrives with the same tone whether it deserves that trust or not. It doesn’t sound less certain when it’s wrong. A non-technical person vibe coding an app has no signal telling them “this part I’m less sure about.” So they extend it the trust Aristotle describes: not because they were careless, but because that is exactly how trust is supposed to work when you can’t verify a claim yourself.
I want to be direct about where this leads, because softening it serves no one: this is structurally similar to being scammed. Not because AI intends deception but because the mechanism is the same one a con relies on. A con works on an asymmetry: the mark can’t verify the claim, and the person making it can. Vibe coding creates that same asymmetry between a non-technical builder and the system they just generated, except nobody is running the con. The asymmetry isn’t malicious. It sits between the speed at which the system generates an answer and the speed at which a person can actually verify it, built into how fluently the tool talks regardless of whether the underlying decision was sound.

Same Phases, Different Bottleneck
It’s tempting to think vibe coding replaced the software development lifecycle. It didn’t. Lay a traditional SDLC beside an AI-driven one and the phases are identical: requirements, design, implementation, testing, review, maintenance. What changed isn’t which phases exist. It’s where the time goes and where the bottleneck now sits.
In the traditional cycle, implementation was the slow, expensive part: one to three weeks of building, resting on requirements and design work that came first.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Source: https://www.kaggle.com/whitepaper-the-new-SDLC-with-vibe-coding?utm_medium=email&utm_source=gamma&utm_campaign=learn-intensive-assignment1-june-2026In the AI-driven cycle, implementation collapses to minutes. But the phases before and after it don’t disappear. They just stop announcing themselves. The bottleneck moves to two places: specification quality, because an agent is only as good as the spec it’s given, and architecture, because a design decision now gets scaffolded at machine speed instead of caught during a slow build.
That second shift is the one non-technical builders miss. Speed doesn’t make architecture less important. It actually makes a bad architectural call more expensive, because it’s extended, built upon, and shipped before anyone would have paused to notice it. In the old cycle, the weeks of implementation were also, quietly, weeks of catching things. Vibe coding removed the weeks and also removed the catching.
The phases are still there. The question is whether anyone is still standing in them.

What the Slow Phases Were Actually For
The diagram shows implementation shrinking from weeks to minutes. What it cannot show is that passing through a phase is not the same as satisfying the judgment that phase existed to enforce.
Technical design wasn’t slow because it is hard. It was slow because it was where someone asked: what parts of this system are allowed to trust each other, and what happens when one of them fails? Testing wasn’t slow because typing test cases takes time. It was slow because it was where someone asked: does this still hold up under the inputs I didn’t imagine while building it? Review wasn’t ceremony. It was the moment a second person, who didn’t write the code, had to understand it well enough to put their name on shipping it.
None of those questions require weeks. They require someone accountable for asking them. That’s the distinction the diagram can’t show and the one that matters most: the traditional SDLC didn’t buy you time, it bought you a sequence of forced checkpoints where a human had to stop and think about failure before it became expensive.

The Skills Nobody Tells Vibe Coders They Need
The obvious prescription is “learn to code.” It’s also the wrong one and not because it’s bad advice, but because it points at the wrong skill. The checkpoints the SDLC used to force weren’t about writing code. They were about judging it. And judgment is a different, narrower, more learnable thing than fluency.
Four capabilities do most of the work, and none of them require building an app from scratch:
Reading output well enough to audit it. Not writing it but recognizing it. You don’t need to author secure code to spot the absence of it.
Systems thinking about trust and data flow. This is the single most valuable literacy in the Age of AI, and it’s closer to architecture than to programming. It’s about the shape of the system, not the syntax.
Knowing what should never be hand-rolled. Authentication, authorization, encryption, access control. The most reliable rule in security is don’t roll your own, and it applies with double force when the thing rolling it doesn’t know it’s rolling it.
Knowing the edge of your own competence. The hardest skill, because it requires seeing the boundary before you cross it. The judgment to recognize when a prototype has quietly become a production system, and to bring in someone who can review the architecture before real data is involved.
None of this is a computer science degree. It’s architectural literacy and it’s exactly the discipline the traditional SDLC forced onto every team, whether they had a formally trained architect in the room or not.

What the Data Now Shows
Until recently, this was an argument you had to make on principle. As of 2026, it’s an argument you can make with numbers.
And it isn’t only non-technical builders who fall into this. The same fluency that convinces a first-time founder convinces experienced engineers too, which is why the evidence below comes from real production code, not toy projects.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Georgia Tech’s Vibe Security Radar traces public vulnerabilities back through code history to the AI that generated them. Its monthly count of such vulnerabilities went from six in January 2026 to fifteen in February to thirty-five in March and the researchers believe the true figure across the wider open-source ecosystem is five to ten times higher than what they can currently detect.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Veracode, testing more than 150 models across some 80 coding tasks, found that roughly 45% of AI-generated code samples introduce a known security flaw with a pass rate that has stayed flat from 2025 through early 2026 even as the same models became far better at writing code that simply compiles. In that same study, one specific class of flaw, which is cross-site scripting, appeared in 86% of the relevant samples.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The most counterintuitive finding is the one that should change how people actually work. A controlled study accepted at IEEE-ISTAS 2025 measured a 37.6% increase in critical vulnerabilities after just five rounds of AI-assisted refinement. Iterating doesn’t clean the code up. It compounds the flaws because each pass optimizes for the functional improvement it was asked for while quietly degrading the security assumptions established earlier. The instinct to keep prompting until it “feels right” is precisely the instinct the data says makes things worse.

The Absence of Doubt, in Code
I’ve written before about AI’s structural inability to signal uncertainty and that it produces the same fluent, confident output whether it’s on solid ground or fabricating, and that a non-expert has no independent way to tell the two apart. Vibe coding is that same problem, wearing different clothes.
The fluency is the danger, because fluency removes the one thing that used to force a pause: friction. This is why the problem isn’t solved by better prompting or a more capable model. Capability isn’t the missing piece. Doubt is and doubt has to be supplied by a human who knows where to place it.
Vibe coding didn’t remove the need for architectural judgment. It removed the signal that judgment was ever required. Aristotle’s insight holds that we trust the confident source when we can’t verify the claim ourselves but he was describing how humans persuade each other, with reputation and accountability attached. And AI borrows that confidence and leaves the accountability behind.
Know that none of this is an argument against building with AI. It’s an argument for knowing where the line sits.
The fun of vibe coding is real. So is the danger.

Sources
Georgia Tech Systems Software & Security Lab, Vibe Security Radar: https://research.gatech.edu/bad-vibes-ai-generated-code-vulnerable-researchers-warn
Veracode, Spring 2026 GenAI Code Security Update: https://www.veracode.com/blog/spring-2026-genai-code-security/
Shukla, Joshi & Syed, “Security Degradation in Iterative AI Code Generation” (IEEE-ISTAS 2025): https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.11022

Marie Patrice Santiago works at the intersection of AI, data, and enterprise transformation across healthcare, insurance, banking, and manufacturing. With a background in mathematics, computer science, and data science, her work focuses on how emerging technologies reshape systems, work, and human behaviour.
